findings.title: Assumption 3 uses unsquared norm ‖b̂‖ where ‖b̂‖² is required
findings.explanation: The prose immediately preceding Assumption 3 correctly identifies the threshold for first-best achievability as the squared norm: 'when the budget is large enough—that is, $C \geq \|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$—the planner can… set $b_i = 0$ for all $i$.' This is verified by the cost function definition $K(\boldsymbol{b},\hat{\boldsymbol{b}})=\sum_i(b_i-\hat{b}_i)^2$: setting $\boldsymbol{b}=\boldsymbol{0}$ costs exactly $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$. However, Assumption 3 writes the exclusion condition as $C<\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|$ (the Euclidean norm, not its square). These two thresholds coincide only when $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|=1$; in general they differ. When $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|>1$ the assumption is over-restrictive (it excludes budgets that would not actually achieve the first-best), and when $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|<1$ it is under-restrictive (it permits budgets that do achieve the first-best). The formal assumption therefore does not correctly rule out the case it is designed to exclude.
findings.fix: Replace $C<\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|$ with $C<\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$ in Assumption 3 to match both the preceding narrative and the cost-function definition.

findings.title: Self-referential change-of-variables formula in Example 2 (missing tilde)
findings.snippet: Performing the change of variables $b_i = [\tau - b_i]/2$ and $\beta = -\tilde{\beta}/2$ (with the status quo equal to $\hat{b}_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$)
findings.explanation: The formula $b_i = [\tau - b_i]/2$ defines $b_i$ in terms of itself, giving the degenerate solution $b_i = \tau/3$, which is not what is intended. The status quo formula immediately following it—$\hat{b}_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$—uses the original utility parameter $\tilde{b}_i$ correctly, confirming that the tilde was accidentally dropped in the change-of-variables line. The FOC derivation for the local-public-goods game confirms the intended mapping: expanding the FOC of $U_i = -\frac{1}{2}(\tau - x_i)^2 - \frac{1}{2}a_i^2$ yields $a_i = \frac{\tau-\tilde{b}_i}{2} - \frac{\tilde{\beta}}{2}\sum_j g_{ij}a_j$, so $b_i$ should equal $\frac{\tau-\tilde{b}_i}{2}$, not $\frac{\tau - b_i}{2}$.
findings.fix: Change $b_i = [\tau - b_i]/2$ to $b_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$ so that the new-variable $b_i$ is expressed in terms of the original utility parameter $\tilde{b}_i$, consistent with the status quo formula and the FOC derivation.

findings.title: Inconsistent notation for eigenvector-basis projections of b̂ within Section 4
findings.explanation: Section 4 uses two distinct typographic forms for what is plainly the same object—the projection of $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ onto the $\ell$th eigenvector $\boldsymbol{u}^\ell(\boldsymbol{G})$. In the reformulated optimization problem the notation $\hat{\underline{b}}_\ell$ (with underline) appears, while in equation (6) and in both the marginal-return and marginal-cost expressions the underline is dropped, yielding $\hat{b}_\ell$. Because neither form is formally defined in these sections, the reader cannot be certain these denote the same scalar quantity without inferring it from context.
findings.fix: Pick one notation (the underlined $\hat{\underline{b}}_\ell$ is preferable because it visually distinguishes the scalar projection from the vector $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$) and apply it uniformly throughout Section 4, including equation (6) and the marginal-condition display.

findings.title: Nonnegativity paragraph mislabels b̂ as 'status quo actions'
findings.snippet: As long as the status quo actions $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ are positive, this constraint will be respected for all $C$ less than some $\hat{C}$
findings.explanation: Throughout both sections $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ is consistently defined as the vector of status quo standalone marginal returns (incentives), not as a vector of actions. The status quo equilibrium actions are $\hat{\boldsymbol{a}}^* = [\boldsymbol{I}-\beta\boldsymbol{G}]^{-1}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$, which in general differ from $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$. Referring to $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ as 'status quo actions' conflates these two objects and is inconsistent with every other usage of $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ in the paper. The condition for equilibrium actions to remain nonneg under a small intervention should in principle be stated in terms of $\hat{\boldsymbol{a}}^*$, not $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$.
findings.fix: Replace 'the status quo actions $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$' with either 'the status quo standalone marginal returns $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$' (if the claim is genuinely about incentives) or 'the status quo equilibrium actions $\hat{\boldsymbol{a}}^* = [\boldsymbol{I}-\beta\boldsymbol{G}]^{-1}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$' (if the claim is about actions), and verify which condition actually guarantees nonnegativity of the post-intervention equilibrium actions.
